title: Toro y Moi Cannot Be Categorized and Hole Erth Proves It
author: Allastair Voss
description: Chazwick Bundick has spent his career making it impossible for anyone to predict what a Toro y Moi album will sound like. Chillwave. House. Funk. Lo-fi
image_alt: Toro y Moi Cannot Be Categorized and Hole Erth Proves It
body:
[spotify:artist/6O4EGCCb6DoIiR6B1QCQgp]

Chazwick Bundick has spent his career making it impossible for anyone to predict what a Toro y Moi album will sound like. Chillwave. House. Funk. Lo-fi pop. R&B. Each record has shifted the coordinates while maintaining something ineffably Chaz about the whole thing. The consistency is not in sound but in sensibility, and that distinction is worth holding onto.

Hole Erth, his eighth studio album, released in September 2024 on Dead Oceans, is perhaps the most extreme pivot yet. Pop-punk energy. Autotuned rap. Collaborations with Don Toliver, Kevin Abstract, and Benjamin Gibbard of Death Cab for Cutie. If you came in expecting Causers of This Part Two, Hole Erth will disorient you. That is the point.

## What Hole Erth Actually Is

The album arrives at a particular cultural moment when the walls between indie, pop, rap, and rock have dissolved to the point where genre descriptors mostly communicate nostalgia rather than sound. Bundick recognizes this and uses it. Hole Erth does not pick a lane. It treats the whole road as available terrain.

The Don Toliver collaboration is the most striking example of this. Toliver operates in a melodic rap space that, on paper, should have nothing to do with the artist who made Underneath the Pine. But the track works because both artists share a fundamental interest in texture and atmosphere, even when the surface aesthetics look completely different. Bundick finds the common ground and builds something that sounds like neither artist at their most typical, which is exactly the right outcome.

The Kevin Abstract collaboration hits differently. Abstract, as a member of BROCKHAMPTON and through his solo work, has spent years at the intersection of indie aesthetics and hip-hop energy. His presence on Hole Erth feels less like a guest spot and more like a conversation between two artists who are solving the same problem from different angles.

Benjamin Gibbard's involvement is the most deliberately incongruous choice on the album, and Bundick knows it. Gibbard is associated with a very specific kind of early-2000s indie rock sincerity. Putting him on a Toro y Moi record in 2024 is a statement about what happens when you strip away genre context and focus on craft. It works because Gibbard is, fundamentally, a strong melodist, and melody is something Bundick has always understood deeply.

## The Production Approach

Bundick has always been his own primary producer, and Hole Erth continues that tradition while pushing into rougher sonic territory than his previous work. The production is deliberately less polished than Mahal, less lush than Underneath the Pine. There is grit here that Bundick has previously kept at a distance.

The pop-punk energy that reviewers noted is real, but it is not a straightforward revival. Bundick is not playing the same nostalgia game that drove much of the mid-2020s return to early-2000s aesthetics. He is using the energy and attack of that sound, the directness of it, without committing to its conventions. The guitars are present. The tempo is there. But the rhythmic and harmonic decisions remain distinctly his own.

Autotune, which Bundick has used before but never this prominently, becomes a compositional tool rather than a corrective one. The pitch-shifted vocals on several tracks are not trying to hide imperfection. They are creating a particular kind of expressiveness that acoustic singing cannot achieve. This is a distinction that matters. Autotune as correction is aesthetically neutral. Autotune as instrument is a choice with implications for how the emotion of a track lands.

## Les Sins and the Side Projects

Bundick's side project Les Sins, his more explicitly dance-oriented alias, has been dormant since a joint EP with AceMo in 2020. The last full-length under that name was Michael in 2014. But the influence of Les Sins bleeds into everything Bundick touches. The rhythmic adventurousness, the willingness to let a groove dictate structure rather than verse-chorus convention, is present across all his work.

The fact that Les Sins has been quiet while Toro y Moi has absorbed its energy suggests that Bundick no longer needs the separation. The main project has become capacious enough to hold everything. This is a sign of artistic maturity. The aliases and side projects that artists use early in their careers to explore without commitment eventually become unnecessary when the primary work expands to accommodate those experiments. Bundick reached that point sometime between Mahal and Hole Erth.

## The Tour and What Comes Next

A winter 2025 North American headline tour with Panda Bear as support ran through February and March, a pairing that made immediate sense: two artists who emerged from the same cultural moment with very different approaches to texture and repetition. An unplugged reworking of Hole Erth, titled Unerthed, was announced for later in 2025.

Bundick also released the Sandhills EP in 2023, a smaller project that served as a bridge between the lush production of Mahal and the rawer energy of Hole Erth. That EP, which drew on his South Carolina roots more explicitly than much of his catalog, functioned as a reset, a reminder that underneath all the genre-shifting was a songwriter with a specific sense of place and feeling.

## The Career

Toro y Moi is one of the most consistently interesting artists in independent music. Not because every album is a masterpiece, but because every album is a genuine attempt to do something he has not done before. In a landscape where artists are rewarded for repeating themselves, where algorithms favor familiarity and catalog consistency, Bundick's refusal to stay still is its own kind of excellence.

The willingness to risk an audience's confusion, to release something like Hole Erth when the comfortable move would have been another record in the mode of Mahal, is increasingly rare. Most artists at Bundick's career stage have identified what their audience expects and settled into providing it reliably. Bundick keeps moving. That is not nothing. In 2024, it is close to everything.
